data.title: European gas prices drop as tariffs and growth fears rattle broader market
data.description:
European natural gas prices (NGEU:COM) extended their losses to Wednesday as U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs triggered demand fears and concerns that a global trade war would dent economic growth.

Benchmark futures fell as much as 7.7% on Wednesday, and are down about 40% from a February peak.

At the end of last week, futures prices for natural gas in Europe dropped sharply by over 10% to €35.2 per MWh, according to the ICE exchange. These are the lowest European gas prices in the past six months.

President Trump’s tariffs on global trade partners took effect on Wednesday, including a 104% levy on China that sets the scene for a dramatic escalation between the world’s two largest economies.

Reciprocal tariffs between Washington and Beijing are prompting China’s LNG buyers to resell U.S. cargoes.

Meanwhile, EU members are reportedly negotiating changes to gas storage targets that could soften this year’s goals amid broader concerns over low inventory levels. 

According to Wood Mackenzie, steep increases announced by Trump and retaliation from other countries point to rising costs and weaker energy demand.

U.S. power industry is one of the sectors that is most exposed to tariff impacts, it said.

The Trump administration is aiming to boost production of energy of all kinds, to support the growth of AI and to bring down costs for consumers. But the tariffs could lead to significant cost increases in the power industry supply chain.

"Uncertainty over tariff liabilities in complex supply chains, and over possible future changes in tariff rates, can also be a deterrent to investment," Wood Mackenzie said.

ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

Major energy companies in Europe include Shell (SHEL), BP (BP), Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY), EDF,  Vestas (OTCPK:VWDRY), Total Energies (TTE), and National Grid.

data.title: European pharma stocks decline as Trump reiterates tariff plans
data.description:
Shares of major European drugmakers fell Wednesday morning after U.S. President Donald Trump reaffirmed plans to impose a "major" tariff on all pharmaceutical imports.

“We’re going to tariff our pharmaceuticals, and once we do that, they are going to come rushing back into our country, because we’re the big market," Trump said during remarks at a dinner in Washington, D.C.

Europe-listed shares of AstraZeneca fell -5.6%, GSK dipped -4.7%, Roche -5.4%, Sanofi -5.4%, argenx SE -4.8%, UCB SA -7.3% and Novartis -6%.

Nasdaq-listed shares of AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) were also down 3% premarket, while GSK (NYSE:GSK) fell 3%, Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) -2.75% and Novartis (NYSE:NVS) dropped -3.5% in the U.S.

Danish drugmaker Novo Nordisk (NVO) fell 1% premarket in the U.S. and 4.7% in Europe.

A basket of European healthcare stocks (SXDP) dropped 4.6%, hitting its lowest level since October 2022 and leading sectoral losses on the pan-European STOXX 600 index (STOXX), which was down nearly 3%.

While Trump has not specified the timing or scale of the proposed tax hikes on pharmaceutical imports, he stated that the tariffs are intended to encourage drug manufacturers to relocate their operations to the U.S.

The President earlier this month said that several large drugmakers -- Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Merck (MRK) -- have made announcements of making manufacturing investments in the U.S. Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Merck (MRK) fell 0.69%, 0.86% and 1.29%, respectively.

data.title: Tariffs wars: China slams Trump's 104% levies, vows retaliation
Stock index futures inch higher as China releases white paper on trade relations
data.description:
Stock index futures inched higher on Wednesday, as China released a white paper seemingly looking to "resolve disputes through dialogue and consultation." 

S&P 500 futures (SPX) +0.2%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +0.9%, and Dow futures (INDU) were flat.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.34%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.77%.

Another topsy-turvy trading session on Wall Street saw the major averages rally furiously at the open, then steadily give up gains and turn negative, followed by a period of fluctuation. They then decidedly turned lower and eventually ended deep in the red.

"Several triggers drove a sharp turn lower in market sentiment as the day went on. First, we had confirmation from U.S. officials that the additional 50% tariff that Trump threatened China with on Monday would be imposed, dashing any remaining hopes for a last-minute extension before the deadline," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

"The market turmoil has shown no sign of letting up over the last 24 hours, with the S&P 500 edging increasingly close to bear market territory, having now shed -18.9% since its peak in mid-February. Moreover, futures are still pointing towards further losses today,"  Allen said.

Trump on Tuesday also said that the U.S. will soon introduce a "major" tariff on pharmaceutical imports.

"Trump implemented an extraordinary tax increase on US consumers overnight. If the 104% tax increase on goods from China is not reversed, it is likely to push the US into recession more rapidly," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

For years, the U.S. persuaded foreigners to exchange U.S. government promises for valuable goods and services. That exchange is threatened—despite equities falling yesterday, bond yields rose sharply, Donovan added.

The results of $39B, 10-year note auction and the FOMC minutes will land later in the day on Wednesday.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Wednesday: BTU, CALM, and more
data.description:
Stock futures were cautious in premarket trading on Wednesday after new tariffs took effect at midnight, signaling a renewed escalation in global trade tensions. China’s foreign ministry reiterated its stance, vowing “resolute and forceful” measures to safeguard national interests, following the implementation of combined U.S. tariffs totaling 104% on certain Chinese imports.

Here are some of Wednesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM) +2.5% – Shares moved higher after Bank of America suggested the recent pullback in TSMC may be overdone, maintaining a Buy rating on the stock despite trimming the price target to $240 from $265. Analyst Brad Lin pointed to tariff concerns and AI-related supply chain hiccups as already factored into the stock, which now trades at attractive valuation levels. Lin expects Q1 revenue to dip 4% sequentially and gross margin to come in near the low end of guidance due to earthquake impacts, but forecasts a rebound in Q2 on stronger AI-driven demand and recovery in shipments. On the policy front, former President Trump warned chipmakers of potential tariffs up to 100% if they don't manufacture in the U.S., criticizing the Biden administration’s $6.6B subsidy to TSMC's Arizona facility. In response to recent 32% U.S. tariffs, Taiwan has offered zero tariffs and more U.S. investments in hopes of reaching a quick resolution.
  • Bath & Body Works (NYSE:BBWI) +4% – Shares rose after Piper Sandler upgraded the stock to Overweight from Neutral, citing strong category growth and solid execution. Analyst Korinne Wolfmeyer noted the business is “fairly well-insulated” from macro pressures, with Fragrance and Body Care among the fastest-growing beauty segments. She highlighted rising penetration in mass channels and set a price target of $35, implying a 37% upside from the last close.
  • Peabody Energy (NYSE:BTU) +17% – Shares extended gains after a 9.2% rise in the regular session, following President Trump’s executive orders aimed at reviving the U.S. coal industry. Trump said he would invoke the Defense Production Act to support coal mining, invest in next-generation coal technologies, and direct the DOJ to challenge state and local regulations impacting coal jobs. He linked the move to bolstering electricity output to maintain U.S. leadership in the AI race.

Biggest stock losers

  • Cal-Maine Foods (NASDAQ:CALM) -7% – Shares declined after the egg producer missed consensus expectations for FQ3, despite reporting a sharp increase in net sales and a more than threefold jump in profit, driven by a significant egg shortage that boosted prices. The company also announced a ~$258M deal to acquire Echo Lake Foods, a Wisconsin-based supplier of ready-to-eat egg and breakfast products. The deal, expected to close by the end of FY25, marks Cal-Maine’s entry into the high-growth, stable foodservice segment of the egg industry.

data.title: IMF, Argentina reach preliminary deal for $20B bailout package
data.description:
• The International Monetary Fund has reached a preliminary agreement with Argentina on a $20 billion bailout, it said on Tuesday, providing relief to President Milei's economic reforms.
  • As a staff-level agreement, the rescue package still requires final approval from the IMF’s executive board. The board will convene in the coming days, the IMF statement said.
  • The program supports the next phase of Argentina’s stabilization and reform agenda aimed at entrenching macroeconomic stability, strengthening external sustainability, and unlocking strong sustainable growth, while also managing the more challenging global backdrop.
  • Argentina's benchmark S&P Merval stock index (MERVAL) has fallen 21% YTD.

data.title: Stallion Uranium increase private placement to raise C$1.45M
European markets back in red with global sell-off sentiment as tariffs took effect
data.description:
London (UKX) -2.15% to 7,740. 

Germany (DAX:IND) -2.29% to 19,815.

France (CAC:IND) -2.28% to 6,938.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) edged 2.98% lower to 472.42, reversing gains from the previous as trade tensions intensified after Trump's sweeping tariffs, including a cumulative 104% levy on Chinese imports. Losses were broad-based, with all sectors in negative territory. Pharmaceuticals, oil & gas, basic resources, and real estate led the declines. Meanwhile, Spain's Prime Minister, Pedro Sánchez, called on the European Union to review its relationship with China.

European natural gas futures hit its lowest level since September as tariffs fueled fears of a global economic slowdown and weaker energy demand.

Coming up in the session: A lighter day on the economic calendar today.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up 12 basis points at 4.38%.

Germany's 10-year yield was down less than 1 basis point at 2.62%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 6 basis points to 4.67%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK).
